words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Paul, an apostle — not from men nor through a man, but through Jesus Christ and God the Father, the one having raised him from the dead —
verses.note: GREEK: Apóstolos (G652) means "one sent forth" — a commissioned agent with full authority of the sender. Paul's emphatic double negation (ouk...oude) stresses his apostleship originates neither collectively from men (anthrōpōn, plural) nor through any individual man (anthrōpou, singular), but directly through Jesus Christ and God the Father. The participle egeírantos ("having raised") ties Paul's commission inseparably to the resurrection event. THEOLOGICAL: By pairing Jesus Christ with God the Father as the dual source of his apostleship, Paul places Christ on the same plane of divine authority as the Father — a profound Christological claim in the letter's very first verse.

verses.flow: and all the brothers with me, to the assemblies of Galatia:
Grace to you and peace from God the Father and our Lord Jesus Christ,

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb perfect active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: the one having given himself on behalf of our sins, so that he might deliver us out of the present evil age, according to the will of our God and Father,
verses.note: GREEK: Exelētai (G1807, aorist middle subjunctive of exairéō) means "to rescue, to pluck out, to deliver." This is a forceful extraction term. The phrase aiōnos tou enestōtos ponērou ("the present evil age") reflects Jewish apocalyptic categories of two ages — the present age under evil dominion and the age to come. THEOLOGICAL: Christ's self-giving is substitutionary (hypér, "on behalf of") and liberating — a rescue operation from the domain of the present evil age. This is accomplished not by human will but katá to thélēma ("according to the will") of God the Father. TYPOLOGY: Echoes the Exodus deliverance pattern — God rescuing his people from bondage (Ex 6:6), now fulfilled in Christ's atoning work delivering from sin's dominion.

verses.flow: to whom be the glory unto the ages of the ages. Amen.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: I marvel that you are so quickly being turned away from the one having called you in the grace of Christ, unto a different gospel,
verses.note: GREEK: Metatíthesthe (G3346) means "to transfer, to transpose, to desert." In secular Greek it described political or philosophical defection. Tachéōs ("so quickly") expresses Paul's astonishment at the speed of their apostasy. Héteron euangélion ("a different gospel") uses héteros (different in kind), not állos (another of the same kind), signaling a qualitative departure. HISTORICAL: The troublers were likely Judaizers who insisted Gentile converts must adopt Torah observance — circumcision, dietary laws, and calendar — for full covenant membership.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: which is not another; except there are some who are troubling you and desiring to pervert the gospel of Christ.
But even if we or an angel out of heaven should proclaim a gospel to you contrary to what we proclaimed to you, let him be accursed.
verses.note: GREEK: Anáthema (G331) derives from the Hebrew ḥērem — that which is devoted to destruction, placed under the ban of God. In the LXX this term denotes what is irrevocably given over to divine judgment (cf. Josh 6:17-18, Deut 7:26). THEOLOGICAL: The gravity of Paul's pronouncement is staggering — he includes even himself (hēmeîs, "we") and heavenly angels under this curse should they alter the gospel. This establishes the gospel's content as transcending all human and angelic authority. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The mention of an angel (ángelos) from heaven potentially distorting God's message echoes the divine council theme where spiritual beings can act contrary to God's purposes (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-23, 2 Cor 11:14).

verses.flow: As we have said before, and now again I say: if anyone proclaims a gospel to you contrary to what you received, let him be accursed.
For am I now persuading men, or God? Or am I seeking to please men? If I were still pleasing men, I would not be a bondservant of Christ.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: For I make known to you, brothers, the gospel that was proclaimed by me, that it is not according to man.
For neither did I receive it from a man, nor was I taught it, but through a revelation of Jesus Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: Apokalýpseōs (G602, from apó + kalýptō, "to unveil, to uncover") denotes a divine disclosure of what was previously hidden. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's gospel came not through human tradition (paralambánō, "receive by transmission") nor through didactic instruction (didáskō, "taught"), but by direct, unmediated revelation from Jesus Christ. The genitive Iēsoû Christoû is likely both subjective (Christ revealed it) and objective (Christ was its content). This parallels the revelation granted to OT prophets directly by God (cf. Amos 3:7, Jer 1:4-5).

verses.flow: For you heard of my former conduct in Judaism, that beyond measure I was persecuting the assembly of God and was destroying it,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and I was advancing in Judaism beyond many contemporaries in my nation, being far more exceedingly zealous for the traditions of my fathers.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when it was well-pleasing to the one having set me apart from the womb of my mother and having called me through his grace,
verses.note: GREEK: Aphorísas (G873) means "to mark off by boundaries, to set apart." TYPOLOGY: Paul's language of being set apart from the womb directly echoes the prophetic calling of Jeremiah (Jer 1:5 LXX) and the Servant of the Lord (Isa 49:1). Paul sees his apostolic commission as standing in continuity with Israel's prophetic tradition — appointed before birth to a specific divine mission. The phrase ek koilías mētros ("from the womb of my mother") underscores God's sovereign, pre-temporal election.

verses.flow: to reveal his Son in me, that I might proclaim him among the nations, immediately I did not confer with flesh and blood,
verses.note: GREEK: Apokalýpsai ton huion autoû en emoí — "to reveal his Son in me." The preposition en may be locative ("within me," an inward revelation) or instrumental ("through me," for proclamation). Both senses likely operate simultaneously. Éthnos (G1484) here means "nations/Gentiles." THEOLOGICAL: The content of divine revelation is not a proposition but a Person — God's Son. Paul's encounter with the risen Christ was simultaneously his conversion and his commissioning to the nations. Sarkí kaí haímati ("flesh and blood") is a Semitic idiom for mere human counsel (cf. Matt 16:17).

verses.flow: nor did I go up to Jerusalem to those who were apostles before me, but I went away into Arabia, and again returned to Damascus.
Then after three years I went up to Jerusalem to visit Cephas, and I remained with him fifteen days.
verses.note: GREEK: Historēsai (G2477) is a rare term meaning "to visit in order to inquire, to get to know personally." This is its only NT occurrence. It implies a purposeful, investigative visit — not to receive apostolic authorization but to become acquainted with Cephas (Kēphâs, Aramaic for "Rock," = Peter). HISTORICAL: The three-year gap after Paul's conversion places this Jerusalem visit around AD 37-38. The fifteen-day stay was brief enough to demonstrate independence yet significant enough to establish fellowship with the chief apostle.

verses.flow: But another of the apostles I did not see, except James the brother of the Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: James (Iákōbon) is identified as ton adelphón toû kyríou — "the brother of the Lord." This designation confirms both the historical existence of Jesus' biological family and James's subsequent rise to leadership in the Jerusalem assembly (cf. Acts 15:13, Acts 21:18). Paul's mention of seeing only Peter and James among the apostles reinforces his argument of independence from the broader apostolic circle. GREEK: The term kýrios (G2962) here applied to Jesus carries its full weight as the title of sovereign authority, echoing its LXX usage for the God of Israel.

verses.flow: Now the things which I write to you, behold, before God, I do not lie.
Then I came into the regions of Syria and of Cilicia.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I was unknown by face to the assemblies of Judea that are in Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But only they were hearing that "The one formerly persecuting us is now proclaiming the faith which he once was destroying,"
verses.note: GREEK: The direct quotation preserves early Christian oral tradition about Paul's dramatic transformation. Pístis (G4102) here means "the faith" — the objective content of Christian belief, not merely subjective trust. Epórthei (G4199, imperfect tense of porthéō) means "was ravaging, was destroying" — the same verb used in v.13 for Paul's persecution. TYPOLOGY: The persecutor becoming proclaimer of the very faith he sought to annihilate serves as a living demonstration of the transformative power of the gospel and of sovereign grace — the ultimate reversal narrative.

verses.flow: and they were glorifying God in me.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Then after fourteen years I went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, having taken along also Titus.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And I went up according to revelation, and I set before them the gospel which I proclaim among the nations—but privately to those who were of repute—lest somehow I should be running, or had run, in vain.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀποκάλυψιν (apokalypsin, Strong's G602) means "revelation" or "unveiling"—Paul stresses that his journey was directed by divine disclosure, not human initiative. The verb ἀνεθέμην (anethemēn, G394) means "to lay before" or "to communicate," a term used for formal consultation. THEOLOGICAL: Paul submits his gospel for examination not out of uncertainty but out of strategic concern that his apostolic labor among the nations not be undermined.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But not even Titus, the one with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised—
yet because of the secretly brought-in false brothers, who crept in to spy out our freedom which we have in Christ Jesus, in order that they might enslave us—
verses.note: GREEK: παρεισάκτους (pareisaktous, G3920) is a rare term meaning "smuggled in, secretly introduced"—a hapax legomenon in the NT. ψευδαδέλφους (pseudadelphous, G5569) means "false brothers." THEOLOGICAL: These infiltrators sought to spy out (κατασκοπῆσαι, G2684, a military espionage term) the freedom believers possess in Christ Jesus, with the aim of re-enslaving them under the law. The language of liberty versus bondage echoes the Exodus typology.

verses.flow: to whom not even for an hour did we yield in subjection, so that the truth of the gospel might continue with you.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But from those who were reputed to be something—whatever they once were matters nothing to me; God does not receive the face of a man—for to me those of repute added nothing.
verses.note: GREEK: πρόσωπον ἀνθρώπου οὐ λαμβάνει is a Semitic idiom from the LXX meaning "God does not show partiality" or "receive the face of a man" (cf. Deut 10:17; Acts 10:34). THEOLOGICAL: Paul invokes divine impartiality to relativize human ecclesiastical authority—the Jerusalem pillars' status adds nothing to his commission from the risen Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But on the contrary, having seen that I had been entrusted with the gospel of the uncircumcision, just as Peter with that of the circumcision—

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for He who worked effectively in Peter for the apostleship of the circumcision worked effectively also in me for the nations—

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and having known the grace that was given to me, James and Cephas and John, those reputed to be pillars, gave the right hand of fellowship to me and to Barnabas, that we should go to the nations, and they to the circumcision;
verses.note: HISTORICAL: James (the brother of the Lord), Cephas (Peter), and John are designated στῦλοι (stȳloi, "pillars," G4769), a term evoking the pillars of the Temple (cf. 1 Kgs 7:21; Rev 3:12). THEOLOGICAL: The right hand of fellowship (δεξιὰς κοινωνίας) formally ratified a recognized division of apostolic labor—Paul and Barnabas to the nations, the Jerusalem apostles to the circumcision—without any subordination of Paul's gospel.

verses.flow: only that we should remember the poor, which very thing I was also eager to do.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when Cephas came to Antioch, I opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Antioch was the first major center of mixed Jewish-Gentile Christian community (Acts 11:19-26). Cephas's visit and subsequent withdrawal created a crisis that threatened the unity of the body of Christ across ethnic lines. GREEK: κατεγνωσμένος (kategnōsmenos, G2607) is a perfect passive participle meaning "having been condemned" or "standing self-condemned"—his own prior conduct condemned him.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For before certain ones came from James, he was eating with the nations; but when they came, he was drawing back and separating himself, fearing those of the circumcision.
And the rest of the Jews also joined him in hypocrisy, so that even Barnabas was carried away with their dissimulation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when I saw that they were not walking uprightly toward the truth of the gospel, I said to Cephas before all, "If you, being a Jew, live in the manner of the nations and not in the manner of the Jews, how do you compel the nations to Judaize?"
verses.note: GREEK: ὀρθοποδοῦσιν (orthopodousin, G3716) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to walk uprightly" or "to walk with straight feet"—a vivid metaphor for doctrinal and ethical consistency. Ἰουδαΐζειν (Ioudaïzein, G2450) is also rare, meaning "to adopt Jewish customs" or "to Judaize." THEOLOGICAL: Paul publicly confronts Peter because the truth of the gospel—that nations are accepted in Christ apart from Torah-observance—was at stake, not merely social etiquette.

verses.flow: "We are Jews by nature and not sinners from among the nations;

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: yet knowing that a man is not justified by works of law, but through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ, even we believed into Christ Jesus, so that we might be justified by the faithfulness of Christ and not by works of law; because by works of law no flesh shall be justified.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ is a genitive construction that may be rendered "faith in Jesus Christ" (objective genitive) or "faithfulness of Jesus Christ" (subjective genitive). The formal equivalence preserves the ambiguity as "faithfulness of," reflecting Christ's own covenant fidelity. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the theological heart of Galatians—justification (δικαιοῦται, G1344) comes not ἐξ ἔργων νόμου ("out of works of law") but through Christ. The final clause echoes Ps 143:2 (LXX 142:2): "no flesh shall be justified." TYPOLOGY: The contrast between law and faith recapitulates the Abraham narrative Paul will develop in ch. 3 (cf. Gen 15:6).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But if, seeking to be justified in Christ, we ourselves also were found to be sinners, is Christ then a minister of sin? May it never be!
For if the things which I tore down, these I build up again, I establish myself as a transgressor.
For I, through law, died to law, so that I might live to God. I have been crucified with Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Through law I died to law" expresses the paradox that the law itself, by exposing sin and pronouncing the curse (cf. Gal 3:13), brought about Paul's death to its regime—so that he might live to God. GREEK: συνεσταύρωμαι (synestaurōmai, G4957) is a perfect passive: "I have been co-crucified with Christ"—the perfect tense indicating an abiding state resulting from a past event. This is the believer's union with Christ in His death.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And it is no longer I who live, but Christ lives in me; and that which I now live in the flesh, I live by the faithfulness of the Son of God, who loved me and gave Himself over on my behalf.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse articulates the doctrine of union with Christ in its most personal and concentrated form. The old self has died with Christ; the new life is Christ Himself living in the believer. GREEK: The phrase τῇ τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ ("of the Son of God") is a high Christological title—Paul grounds daily existence in the faithfulness of One who is fully divine. The participles ἀγαπήσαντός (having loved) and παραδόντος (having given over) point to the cross as the supreme act of love (cf. Rom 8:32; Eph 5:2, 25).

verses.flow: I do not set aside the grace of God; for if righteousness comes through law, then Christ died for nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's closing statement forms a devastating logical conclusion: if righteousness (δικαιοσύνη, G1343) could come through law, then Christ's death was δωρεάν (dōrean, G1432)—"gratuitously," "for nothing," "without cause." To revert to law-righteousness is to nullify (ἀθετέω, G114) the grace of God and render the cross purposeless. This directly challenges Peter's behavior and the Judaizers' theology.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: O foolish Galatians! Who has bewitched you, before whose eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified?
verses.note: GREEK: "Bewitched" (ἐβάσκανεν, ebaskanen, Strong's G940) refers to casting the evil eye or a spell — a vivid metaphor for the persuasive power of the Judaizing teachers. "Publicly portrayed" (προεγράφη, proegraphē, G4270) means to write or placard before the public eye, as in a public notice; Paul's preaching made the crucifixion visually vivid to them.

verses.flow: This only I wish to learn from you: did you receive the Spirit from works of law, or from hearing of faith?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul frames the entire debate around the reception of the Spirit as empirical evidence. "Hearing of faith" (ἀκοῆς πίστεως) can mean either "the hearing that comes from faith" or "the message that produces faith" — both readings underscore that the Spirit came apart from Torah observance. This directly parallels the Pentecost experience in Acts 2 and the Gentile reception in Acts 10:44-48.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Are you so foolish? Having begun in the Spirit, are you now being made complete in the flesh?
So many things you suffered in vain — if indeed it was even in vain.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: He then who supplies to you the Spirit and works powers among you, does He do so from works of law or from hearing of faith?
Just as Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Genesis 15:6 (LXX) — the foundational text establishing that Abraham was declared righteous by faith prior to circumcision (Gen 17) and prior to the giving of the law (Ex 19-20). This becomes the paradigmatic pattern: covenant relationship with God is established through trust, not through works of Torah. GREEK: ἐλογίσθη (elogisthē, G3049) is an accounting term meaning "reckoned" or "credited to one's account."

verses.flow: Know then that those who are of faith, these are sons of Abraham.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the Scripture, having foreseen that God would justify the nations from faith, proclaimed the good news beforehand to Abraham: "In you shall all the nations be blessed."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Scripture, having foreseen" (προϊδοῦσα ἡ γραφή) personifies Scripture as a prophetic agent, identifying it with the voice of God Himself. The quotation combines Genesis 12:3 and 18:18 (LXX). TYPOLOGY: The promise to Abraham that "all the nations" (πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) would be blessed is the proto-evangelion of Gentile inclusion, fulfilled in the mission of Christ and the apostolic proclamation.

verses.flow: So then, those who are of faith are blessed with the faithful Abraham.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural neuter

verses.flow: For as many as are of works of law are under a curse; for it is written: "Cursed is everyone who does not abide in all things written in the book of the law, to do them."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 27:26 (LXX) to demonstrate that the law-system demands total, unbroken obedience — anything less places one under a covenantal curse. This is not an argument against the law's goodness but against its sufficiency as a means of justification. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The curse (κατάρα, katara) invokes the covenant lawsuit motif of Deuteronomy 28-32, where heaven and earth are called as witnesses against Israel.

verses.flow: Now that no one is justified by law before God is evident, because "The righteous one shall live from faith."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the law is not of faith; rather, "The one who has done them shall live by them."

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, having become a curse on our behalf, because it is written: "Cursed is everyone who hangs upon a tree,"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 21:23 (LXX). Christ becomes the embodiment of the Torah's curse in a substitutionary act — "having become a curse on our behalf" (γενόμενος ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν κατάρα). The "tree" (ξύλον, xylon, G3586) connects to the cross and echoes both the cursed tree of Eden and the bronze serpent lifted on a pole (Num 21:8-9; John 3:14). GREEK: ἐξηγόρασεν (exēgorasen, G1805) means "bought out of the marketplace" — a slave-redemption term.

verses.flow: in order that the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations in Christ Jesus, in order that we might receive the promise of the Spirit through faith.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: Brothers, I speak according to a human example: even a ratified covenant of a man, no one sets aside or adds conditions to.
Now to Abraham were the promises spoken, and to his seed. He does not say, "And to seeds," as concerning many, but as concerning one: "And to your seed," who is Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: Paul makes a grammatical argument based on the singular σπέρματι (spermati, "seed," G4690) in Genesis 13:15, 17:8 (LXX). While "seed" is naturally a collective singular in both Hebrew (זֶרַע) and Greek, Paul reads it christologically as pointing to one ultimate descendant. TYPOLOGY: Christ is the singular Seed of Abraham in whom all the covenant promises find their fulfillment and through whom the Abrahamic blessing flows to all nations (Gen 22:18; 2 Sam 7:12-14).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And this I say: a covenant previously ratified by God, the law which came four hundred and thirty years later does not annul, so as to abolish the promise.
For if the inheritance is from law, it is no longer from promise; but to Abraham, God has granted it through promise.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Why then the law? It was added on account of transgressions, until the seed should come to whom it had been promised, having been ordained through angels by the hand of a mediator.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The law was "ordained through angels" (διαταγεὶς δι' ἀγγέλων) — reflecting a widespread Second Temple Jewish tradition (cf. Acts 7:38, 53; Heb 2:2; Jubilees 1:27-29; Josephus, Ant. 15.136) that angelic beings mediated the Sinai covenant. This places the law at one remove from God's direct promise to Abraham, establishing its secondary and temporary role. GREEK: μεσίτης (mesitēs, G3316) refers to a go-between or intermediary, traditionally understood as Moses.

verses.flow: Now a mediator is not of one, but God is one.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Is the law then against the promises of God? May it never be! For if a law had been given that was able to give life, then indeed righteousness would have been from law.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: But the Scripture shut up all things under sin, in order that the promise from faith in Jesus Christ might be given to those who believe.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Scripture shut up all things under sin" (συνέκλεισεν ἡ γραφὴ τὰ πάντα ὑπὸ ἁμαρτίαν) means the law functioned to imprison the entire cosmos under the diagnosis of sin, so that the only available exit would be the promise realized through faith in Jesus Christ. GREEK: συνέκλεισεν (synekleisen, G4788) means "enclosed together" or "locked up on all sides" — emphasizing the comprehensive and inescapable nature of sin's dominion apart from Christ.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: But before faith came, we were kept in custody under law, being shut up unto the faith about to be revealed.
So that the law has become our guardian unto Christ, in order that from faith we might be justified.
verses.note: GREEK: παιδαγωγός (paidagōgos, G3807) was not a "teacher" but a household slave who escorted children to school and supervised their conduct — a custodian with disciplinary authority. The term implies both protective custody and temporary subordination. THEOLOGICAL: The law's role was provisional, leading to Christ (εἰς Χριστόν) — either "until Christ came" (temporal) or "toward Christ" (teleological). Both senses are likely intended: the law both pointed forward to and gave way to Christ.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: But faith having come, we are no longer under a guardian.
For you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus.
For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.
There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is not male and female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most sweeping statements of new-creation unity in the New Testament. The three pairs — Jew/Greek, slave/free, male/female — correspond to fundamental social divisions in the ancient world. The phrase "not male and female" (οὐκ ἔνι ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ) deliberately echoes Genesis 1:27 LXX, suggesting that in Christ a new humanity is inaugurated that transcends even the primal created distinctions in terms of standing before God. STRUCTURE: The shift from οὐδέ ("nor") in the first two pairs to καί ("and") in the third pair signals the direct Genesis allusion.

verses.flow: And if you are of Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, heirs according to promise.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The chapter's argument reaches its climax: Gentile believers who belong to Christ are thereby Abraham's seed and heirs of the covenant promise — not through Torah observance or ethnic lineage but through union with the singular Seed, Christ (v. 16). THEOLOGICAL: κληρονόμοι (klēronomoi, G2818) — "heirs" — carries the full weight of Israel's inheritance language, now applied to all who are in Christ, fulfilling the Abrahamic covenant's universal scope (Gen 12:3; Rom 4:13-16).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now I say, for as long a time as the heir is a child, he differs nothing from a slave, though being lord of all,
but he is under guardians and stewards until the appointed time of the father.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: So also we, when we were children, were held in bondage under the elements of the world.
verses.note: GREEK: "stoicheia tou kosmou" (στοιχεῖα τοῦ κόσμου, Strong's G4747) — "elements of the world" — refers to rudimentary principles or elemental forces. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In Second Temple Judaism, this phrase carried connotations of spiritual powers or beings governing the nations (cf. Deut 32:8 LXX; Dan 10:13), suggesting that Torah observance apart from Christ places one under the dominion of lesser spiritual authorities rather than the Most High God.

words.g: which is put in to fill up

words.m: verb aorist middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when the fullness of the time came, God sent forth His Son, having come into being from a woman, having come into being under the Law,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The fullness of the time" (τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ χρόνου) marks God's sovereignly appointed moment in redemptive history. "Sent forth" (ἐξαπέστειλεν, G1821) implies pre-existence and divine commissioning — the Son was sent from the Father's presence. "Having come into being from a woman" (γενόμενον ἐκ γυναικός) affirms true incarnation. TYPOLOGY: Echoes Genesis 3:15 — the seed of the woman — and fulfills the promise that through Abraham's seed all nations would be blessed (Gen 12:3; Gal 3:16).

verses.flow: in order that He might redeem those under the Law, in order that we might receive the adoption as sons.
verses.note: GREEK: "Redeem" (ἐξαγοράσῃ, G1805) means to buy out of the marketplace — a slave-purchase metaphor indicating complete liberation. "Adoption as sons" (υἱοθεσίαν, G5206) is a Roman legal term for the formal placement of an heir into full sonship rights. THEOLOGICAL: The double purpose clause ("in order that...in order that") shows Christ's redemptive work has both a negative aim (liberation from Law's curse) and a positive aim (bestowal of filial status).

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And because you are sons, God sent forth the Spirit of His Son into our hearts, crying out, "Abba! Father!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sending of the Spirit parallels the sending of the Son in v.4, establishing a Trinitarian pattern: God the Father sends the Son and the Spirit. "Abba" (Ἀββᾶ) is Aramaic for "Father" — an intimate familial address. GREEK: "Crying out" (κρᾶζον, G2896) is a strong, loud exclamation, suggesting the Spirit's testimony within believers is not quiet assent but powerful, confident proclamation of filial identity (cf. Rom 8:15-16).

verses.flow: So that you are no longer a slave but a son; and if a son, then also an heir through God.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: But then indeed, not knowing God, you were in bondage to those which by nature are not gods.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Those which by nature are not gods" (τοῖς φύσει μὴ οὖσι θεοῖς) — Paul acknowledges that the Galatians' former pagan worship was directed at beings that exist but are not truly God by nature. This aligns with the divine council worldview where lesser spiritual beings (elohim) received the nations' worship (Deut 32:17; Ps 96:5 LXX), yet are ontologically inferior to the one true God.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But now, having come to know God — or rather, having become known by God — how is it that you turn back again to the weak and beggarly elements, to which again from the beginning you desire to be in bondage?
verses.note: GREEK: "Weak and beggarly elements" (ἀσθενῆ καὶ πτωχὰ στοιχεῖα) — Paul stunningly equates returning to Torah-observance as a covenant requirement with returning to pagan worship, since both place one under the stoicheia. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "having become known by God" (γνωσθέντες ὑπὸ θεοῦ) corrects any notion that human initiative drives salvation — it is God's sovereign knowing and choosing that is primary (cf. 1 Cor 8:3).

verses.flow: You observe days and months and seasons and years.
I fear for you, lest perhaps in vain I have labored for you.
Become as I am, for I also became as you, brothers; I beseech you. You did me no wrong.
But you know that because of a weakness of the flesh I proclaimed the good news to you the former time,
and the trial to you in my flesh you did not despise nor reject with contempt, but as an angel of God you received me, as Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Reject with contempt" translates ἐξεπτύσατε (ekeptysate, G1609) — literally "spit out," a hapax legomenon in the NT. In the ancient world, spitting was an apotropaic gesture to ward off evil or disease. HISTORICAL: Paul's bodily affliction evidently could have provoked revulsion, yet the Galatians received him as a messenger (angelos) of God — indeed as Christ Jesus Himself, showing the highest conceivable honor.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Where then is your blessedness? For I bear witness to you that, if possible, you would have plucked out your own eyes and given them to me.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, have I become your enemy by speaking truth to you?
They are zealous for you, not rightly, but they desire to shut you out, in order that you might be zealous for them.
But it is good to be zealously sought in a good thing always, and not only when I am present with you.
My children, for whom again I travail in birth until Christ be formed in you —
verses.note: GREEK: "Travail in birth" (ὠδίνω, G5605) — Paul uses maternal imagery for his apostolic labor. "Until Christ be formed (μορφωθῇ, G3445) in you" — morphoō implies the inward shaping of Christ's very nature within believers. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most profound statements of Paul's understanding of sanctification: it is not merely behavioral conformity but the ontological formation of Christ's character and life within the believing community.

verses.flow: but I was wishing to be present with you now, and to change my tone, for I am perplexed about you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Tell me, you who desire to be under the Law, do you not hear the Law?
For it is written that Abraham had two sons: one from the slave woman and one from the free woman.
But the one from the slave woman has been born according to the flesh, and the one from the free woman through the promise.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Which things are being spoken allegorically; for these are two covenants: one from Mount Sinai, bearing children unto bondage, which is Hagar.
verses.note: GREEK: "Being spoken allegorically" (ἀλληγορούμενα, G238) — the only use of this term in the NT. Paul does not deny the historicity of the Genesis account but discerns a deeper typological correspondence. TYPOLOGY: Hagar-Sinai-present Jerusalem represents the covenant of law producing bondage; Sarah-promise-heavenly Jerusalem represents the covenant of grace producing freedom. The two covenants correspond to two ages of redemptive history.

verses.flow: Now Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia, and she corresponds to the present Jerusalem, for she is in bondage with her children.
But the Jerusalem above is free, which is our mother.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Jerusalem above" (ἡ ἄνω Ἱερουσαλήμ) reflects Jewish apocalyptic expectation of a heavenly city prepared by God (cf. Heb 12:22; Rev 21:2). Paul identifies this free, heavenly Jerusalem as the true mother of all believers, both Jew and Gentile. TYPOLOGY: This corresponds to Sarah, the free woman, and to the new covenant promise — the eschatological city is present reality for those in Christ, not merely a future hope.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For it is written: "Rejoice, O barren one, you who do not bear; break forth and cry aloud, you who do not travail; for many are the children of the desolate, more than of her who has the husband."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Quoted from Isaiah 54:1 (LXX), originally addressed to barren Zion after exile. Paul applies it to the new covenant community — the formerly "barren" Gentile church now produces more children than the synagogue under the old covenant. The prophetic oracle thus finds its ultimate fulfillment in the ingathering of the nations through Christ.

verses.flow: Now you, brothers, according to Isaac, are children of promise.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But just as then the one born according to the flesh persecuted the one born according to the Spirit, so also it is now.
But what does the Scripture say? "Cast out the slave woman and her son, for the son of the slave woman shall by no means inherit with the son of the free woman."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Quoted from Genesis 21:10 — the words of Sarah to Abraham. Paul treats Scripture itself as the speaking authority ("what does the Scripture say?"). The expulsion of Ishmael typologically prefigures the ultimate incompatibility of law-based covenant membership with promise-based inheritance. THEOLOGICAL: The inheritance belongs exclusively to the children of the free woman — those who are in Christ by faith, not those who seek justification through Torah observance.

verses.flow: Therefore, brothers, we are not children of a slave woman, but of the free woman.
For the freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not again be held fast in a yoke of slavery.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐλευθερίᾳ" (eleutheria, Strong's G1657) denotes freedom or liberty, here a dative of advantage or purpose. "στήκετε" (stēkete, G4739) is a late Koine form meaning "stand firm," carrying the sense of resolute posture. The verse forms a hinge between Paul's allegory of Sarah and Hagar (ch. 4) and the practical exhortation that follows. THEOLOGICAL: Christian freedom is not self-achieved but granted by Christ's redemptive work; returning to Torah-observance for justification is reimposing the Sinai covenant yoke (Acts 15:10).

verses.flow: Behold, I, Paul, say to you that if you receive circumcision, Christ will profit you nothing.

words.m: verb present passive participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I testify again to every man being circumcised that he is a debtor to do the whole law.
You have been severed from Christ, you who would be justified by law; you have fallen away from grace.
verses.note: GREEK: "κατηργήθητε" (katērgēthēte, G2673) is aorist passive of katargeō, meaning "to be rendered ineffective, severed, nullified." "ἐξεπέσατε" (exepesate, G1601) means "to fall out of, fall away from." THEOLOGICAL: Paul does not describe loss of salvation per se but a functional severance — those seeking justification through Torah have displaced themselves from the sphere of grace wherein Christ operates. The two systems of justification are mutually exclusive.

verses.flow: For we, by the Spirit, out of faith, eagerly await the hope of righteousness.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision avails anything nor uncircumcision, but faith working through love.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐνεργουμένη" (energoumenē, G1754) is a middle/passive participle of energeō — faith that is "being made effective" or "working itself out" through love. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is pivotal in the faith-works debate. Paul affirms that saving faith is not inert but is actuated through love (agapē), bridging the seeming tension between Paul and James (Jas 2:17-26). Neither the covenant sign of circumcision nor its absence has power; only faith expressing itself through love avails.

verses.flow: You were running well; who hindered you from obeying the truth?

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: This persuasion is not from the One who calls you.
A little leaven leavens the whole lump.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul draws on the Passover imagery of leaven removal (Exod 12:15; 1 Cor 5:6-8). In Jewish practice, leaven was purged before Passover as a symbol of corruption. Here, the "little leaven" represents the false teaching of the Judaizers — even a small doctrinal corruption spreads through the whole community.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I have confidence in you in the Lord, that you will think nothing other; but the one troubling you shall bear the judgment, whoever he may be.
But I, brothers, if I still preach circumcision, why am I still persecuted? Then the stumbling-block of the cross has been abolished.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Would that those who are unsettling you would even cut themselves off!
verses.note: GREEK: "ἀποκόψονται" (apokopsontai, G609) means "to cut off" — Paul uses biting irony: if the agitators are so zealous for circumcision, let them go further and mutilate themselves entirely. HISTORICAL: This likely alludes to the castration rites of the Cybele cult priests (Galli) well known in Asia Minor, making the sarcasm particularly sharp for a Galatian audience.

verses.flow: For you were called unto freedom, brothers; only do not use the freedom as an opportunity for the flesh, but through love serve one another.
For the whole law is fulfilled in one word, in this: "You shall love your neighbor as yourself."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Lev 19:18 (LXX: "Ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν"), the same command Jesus identified as the second greatest commandment (Matt 22:39; Mark 12:31). "πεπλήρωται" (peplērōtai, G4137) is perfect passive — the law stands fulfilled, completed in its deepest intent through this single word. THEOLOGICAL: The law finds its telos not in ritual observance but in self-giving love — the very principle embodied in Christ's cross.

verses.flow: But if you bite and devour one another, take heed that you are not consumed by one another.
But I say, walk by the Spirit, and you shall by no means fulfill the desire of the flesh.
For the flesh desires against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh; for these are opposed to one another, so that you may not do the things that you would.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The flesh-Spirit antithesis is not Greek dualism of body versus soul but reflects Paul's eschatological framework: the old age (sarx) and the new age (pneuma) overlap in the present. The believer lives at the intersection of these two ages. "ἀντίκειται" (antikeitai, G480) means "to be set against, opposed" — the two are in irreconcilable warfare. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The cosmic conflict between the Spirit of God and the domain of the flesh mirrors the broader spiritual warfare between God's purposes and rebellious powers (Eph 6:12).

verses.flow: But if you are led by the Spirit, you are not under law.
Now the works of the flesh are manifest, which are: sexual immorality, uncleanness, licentiousness,
idolatry, sorcery, enmities, strife, jealousy, outbursts of wrath, rivalries, divisions, factions,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: envyings, drunkenness, revelries, and things like these, of which I forewarn you, even as I said before, that those who practice such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "κληρονομήσουσιν" (klēronomēsousin, G2816) means "shall inherit" — a term rooted in Israel's inheritance of the Promised Land, now applied to the eschatological kingdom of God. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's vice list echoes standard Hellenistic and Jewish moral catalogs but is grounded in the warning that persistent, unrepentant practice of these works excludes one from God's kingdom inheritance (1 Cor 6:9-10; Eph 5:5).

verses.flow: But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness,
verses.note: GREEK: "καρπός" (karpos, G2590) is singular — "fruit," not "fruits." This is deliberate: the Spirit produces a unified cluster of virtues, not isolated traits. THEOLOGICAL: In contrast to the plural "works" (erga) of the flesh, the singular "fruit" emphasizes the organic unity of the Spirit's transformative work. These nine qualities reflect the character of Christ Himself and are produced by the indwelling Spirit, not by human moral effort.

verses.flow: gentleness, self-control; against such things there is no law.
And those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐσταύρωσαν" (estaurōsan, G4717) is aorist active — "have crucified," a decisive, completed action. TYPOLOGY: The believer's crucifixion of the flesh participates in Christ's own crucifixion (Gal 2:20). The "passions" (pathēmasin, G3804) and "desires" (epithymiais, G1939) represent the totality of the fallen nature's drives. This is the existential application of the cross — not merely a doctrine believed but a reality enacted in the believer's life.

verses.flow: If we live by the Spirit, let us also walk by the Spirit.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let us not become vainglorious, provoking one another, envying one another.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Brothers, even if a man be overtaken in some trespass, you who are spiritual, restore such a one in a spirit of gentleness, watching yourself, lest you also be tempted.
verses.note: GREEK: "Prolēmphthē" (προλημφθῇ, from prolambánō) means "to be overtaken" or "caught beforehand," suggesting being surprised by sin rather than deliberate transgression. "Katartizete" (καταρτίζετε, Strong's G2675) means "to mend, restore, put in order" — the same word used for mending nets in Matt 4:21, carrying a sense of careful repair rather than punishment. THEOLOGICAL: The pneumatikoi ("spiritual ones") are those walking by the Spirit as described in Gal 5:16-25, now given pastoral responsibility.

verses.flow: Bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The law of Christ" (ton nomon tou Christou) is a striking phrase in a letter arguing against Torah-observance for justification. Paul redefines "law" around Christ's own command of love (Gal 5:14; John 13:34), showing that freedom from the Mosaic covenant does not mean lawlessness but a higher fulfillment. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the love commandment of Lev 19:18 now fulfilled in and through the Messiah.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For if anyone thinks himself to be something, being nothing, he deceives himself.
But let each one prove his own work, and then in himself alone he shall have his boasting, and not in the other.
For each one shall bear his own load.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phortion" (φορτίον, Strong's G5413) differs from "barē" (βάρη, burdens) in v2. "Barē" suggests crushing, oppressive weights shared communally, while "phortion" denotes one's own individual load of responsibility — as of a soldier's pack. The two verses are complementary, not contradictory.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And let the one being instructed in the word share with the one instructing in all good things.
Be not deceived: God is not mocked. For whatever a man sows, this also he shall reap.
verses.note: GREEK: "Myktērizetai" (μυκτηρίζεται, Strong's G3456) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning literally "to turn up the nose at, to sneer at, to mock." It derives from "myktēr" (nose). THEOLOGICAL: Paul states a universal divine principle of sowing and reaping, echoing Prov 22:8 (LXX) and Hos 8:7, grounding ethical exhortation in the character of God who cannot be outwitted.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one sowing unto his own flesh shall from the flesh reap corruption, but the one sowing unto the Spirit shall from the Spirit reap life eternal.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The flesh/Spirit antithesis here summarizes the entire argument of Galatians 5-6. "Phthorá" (corruption/decay) stands in absolute contrast to "zōēn aiōnion" (life eternal). The eschatological harvest imagery connects to Dan 12:2 and the final resurrection, making present moral choices carry eternal weight.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And let us not grow weary in doing good, for in its own season we shall reap, if we do not lose heart.
So then, as we have opportunity, let us work the good toward all, and especially toward those of the household of the faith.
See with what large letters I have written to you with my own hand.
As many as desire to make a fair show in the flesh, these compel you to be circumcised, only that they may not be persecuted for the cross of Christ.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For not even those who are being circumcised themselves keep the law, but they desire you to be circumcised, that in your flesh they may boast.
But far be it from me to boast, except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the climactic confession of the epistle. "Estaurōtai" (ἐσταύρωται, perfect passive) indicates a completed crucifixion with abiding results — the world stands permanently crucified to Paul, and he to the world. The cross is not merely a historical event but an ongoing reality reshaping all cosmic allegiances. TYPOLOGY: Paul's union with Christ's cross fulfills his earlier declaration: "I have been crucified with Christ" (Gal 2:20).

verses.flow: For neither is circumcision anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Kainē ktisis" (καινὴ κτίσις, "new creation") is the theological summit of Galatians. Neither Jewish identity marker (circumcision) nor Gentile status (uncircumcision) holds any significance — only the new creation inaugurated by Christ's death and resurrection. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Isa 65:17 and 66:22 (new heavens and new earth) and anticipates the consummation of 2 Cor 5:17 and Rev 21:1-5.

verses.flow: And as many as shall walk by this rule, peace be upon them, and mercy, even upon the Israel of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Israel of God" (ton Israēl tou theou) is a debated phrase. In context, Paul likely refers to the true, eschatological Israel composed of all who walk by the rule of new creation — both believing Jews and Gentiles united in Christ. The "kaí" before "upon the Israel of God" may be epexegetical ("that is, the Israel of God"), identifying this community as the covenant people redefined around faith in the Messiah. The blessing echoes Ps 125:5 and Ps 128:6 (LXX).

verses.flow: Henceforth let no one cause me troubles, for I bear in my body the marks of Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Stigmata" (στίγματα, Strong's G4742) refers to brand-marks, as on slaves or devotees of a deity. HISTORICAL: In the Roman world, slaves and soldiers bore brands of ownership. Paul's scars from persecution (2 Cor 11:23-25) serve as visible proof of his belonging to Jesus as Lord, trumping any mark of circumcision that the agitators demand.

verses.flow: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers. Amen.
